name: Interview Prep
blurb: Data/analyst interviews, cracked — with the lines, the probes and the gotchas.

lessons.title: The Narrative Round: 90-Second Pitch, STAR, and the AI Question
lessons.concept:
The interview opens with "tell me about yourself," and this is not a cue to recite your resume from Class 10 onwards. It is a 90-second pitch built on three beats: Present (what you own now and one metric you moved), Path (the one or two moves that got you here, framed as deliberate, not accidental), and Payoff (why this role is the logical next step). A Gurugram or Bengaluru panel hears twenty versions of "I did B.Tech, then joined an IT services firm." What separates you is signal, a number you moved, a tool you truly own, a business you actually understood, and ending your pitch by pointing at THIS role, which reads as intent rather than desperation.

The behavioural block runs on STAR: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Keep Situation and Task to one sentence each; spend your words on Action (what YOU did, so say "I," not "we") and Result (a number and what changed permanently). Prepare four stories cold before you walk in: a win, a failure, a conflict, and a deadline. Indian panels lean hard on the failure and conflict prompts because they screen for humility and for people who can disagree with a manager without it becoming drama.

In 2026 every panel asks how you use AI, and the honest senior answer is calibrated. Name where AI genuinely speeds you up, boilerplate SQL, a first draft of a DAX measure, docstrings, and name where you still own the judgment: validating outputs, checking a join grain, reading the business. Claiming you never touch it reads as dishonest or out of touch; claiming it does everything reads as junior. Calibrated honesty, where you can state the exact failure mode you guard against, wins the room.
lessons.example:
TELL ME ABOUT YOURSELF
Line: "I'm a data analyst at [X], where I own the retention dashboards for the used-car buying funnel. Last year I cut weekly reporting from 6 hours to 20 minutes by moving us off Excel onto Power BI, and I found a leak at the test-drive-to-booking step that recovered about 4% of conversions. I came from operations, which is why I read numbers as decisions, not just charts. I want to go deeper into product analytics, which is exactly this role."
Probe: "Tell me more about that 4% leak, how did you find it?"
Gotcha: Rambling past 90 seconds or opening with "So I was born in...". Senior signal: closing your pitch by pointing at THIS role.

STAR (failure)
Line: "I shipped a churn dashboard with the wrong join grain, orders joined to users double-counted revenue, and a stakeholder caught it in review. I had to rebuild trust, not just the query. I rebuilt it on a validated grain, added a row-count reconciliation check, and walked the team through it. That check is now standard on every dashboard we ship."
Probe: "What would you do differently before shipping next time?"
Gotcha: A 'failure' that is a secret humblebrag ('I care too much'). Senior signal: a real mistake plus the systemic fix that stops it recurring.

AI HONESTY
Line: "I use AI to draft SQL and DAX and to speed up documentation, but I never trust an output I haven't validated, I always check the join grain and sanity-check totals against a known number."
Probe: "Tell me about a time AI gave you a wrong answer."
Gotcha: "I don't use AI." Senior signal: naming the specific failure mode, hallucinated columns, wrong grain, plausible-but-wrong totals.
lessons.code:
# 90-second opener = Present -> Path -> Payoff
Present:  "I'm a [role] at [company]; I own [scope] and moved [metric]."
Path:     "I got here via [1-2 deliberate moves]; that's why I bring [lens]."
Payoff:   "I want to [growth], which is exactly what this role is."

# STAR: keep S+T to one line each, spend on A+R
Situation -> 1 line of context
Task      -> 1 line: what YOU were on the hook for
Action    -> 3-4 lines: YOUR moves, say "I" not "we"
Result    -> 1 line: a NUMBER + what changed permanently

# Prep 4 stories cold: WIN | FAILURE | CONFLICT | DEADLINE
lessons.tryit: Write and time your own 90-second pitch out loud using Present -> Path -> Payoff. Then draft all four STAR stories (win, failure, conflict, deadline) as five lines each. Record yourself; if the pitch runs over 90 seconds, cut the Path beat first, not the Payoff.

mistakes.m: Answering 'tell me about yourself' with a full chronological history starting from school.
mistakes.fix: Use Present -> Path -> Payoff and stop near 90 seconds; let the panel probe whatever interests them.

mistakes.m: Telling STAR stories in 'we' so the panel can't tell what YOU actually did.
mistakes.fix: Say 'I' for every Action step; reserve 'we' for genuine shared context only.

mistakes.m: Claiming you never use AI, or that AI does all your analysis for you.
mistakes.fix: Give the calibrated answer: where it speeds you up AND the specific outputs you always validate (grain, totals).

quiz.q: What is the right shape for a 'tell me about yourself' answer?
opts: A full chronological history starting from school
A ~90-second Present -> Path -> Payoff pitch ending on the role
A list of every tool and certification you hold
Your salary expectations up front
quiz.why: The opener is a tight pitch, not a biography: Present (current scope plus a metric), Path (why you're here), Payoff (why this role). Ending by pointing at the role signals intent, and keeping it near 90 seconds leaves room for the panel to probe.
quiz.approach: Present -> Path -> Payoff, about 90 seconds, close on the role.

quiz.q: In a STAR answer, where should most of your words go?
opts: Situation, to set rich context
Task, to explain the assignment
Action (what YOU did) and Result (a number)
The company's background and industry
quiz.why: Situation and Task are just one-line context. The interviewer is grading your behaviour and impact, which live in the Action (spoken in first person, 'I') and a quantified Result that shows what changed.
quiz.approach: Trim S and T, spend on A and R, say 'I', end on a number.

quiz.q: What is the strongest answer to 'how do you use AI in your work?'
opts: I never use AI
AI does all my analysis for me
I use it to draft SQL/DAX and docs, but validate every output (grain, totals)
I'd rather not say
quiz.why: Calibrated honesty is the senior signal: naming where AI accelerates you AND the specific things you always verify shows judgment. Denying use reads as dishonest or out of touch, and over-claiming reads as junior.
quiz.approach: Name the speed-up AND the validation step you personally own.

lessons.title: The SQL Screen: The 5 Patterns Interviewers Reuse
lessons.concept:
The SQL screen for analyst roles in India is remarkably predictable. Whether it is a fintech like Razorpay, a marketplace like Swiggy, or a bank's analytics team, the same five shapes recur, and if you learn them cold you clear roughly four out of five screens. The unifying senior habit is to say the grain out loud before writing anything: "one row per what, order or order-item?" and to ask how to treat ties and NULLs. Interviewers grade the questions you ask almost as much as the query you write.

Four of the five patterns lean on window functions, a value computed OVER a window of rows defined by PARTITION BY (the group) and ORDER BY (the sequence). The mental model: PARTITION BY means "reset the counter for each user or entity," ORDER BY means "in what order." Latest-row-per-entity uses ROW_NUMBER filtered to 1; second-highest uses DENSE_RANK filtered to 2; month-over-month uses LAG to reach the previous row; and the funnel uses conditional aggregation, counting distinct users who reached each step. The fifth pattern, duplicate detection, is plain GROUP BY ... HAVING.

The single most common trap is second-highest. Candidates reach for ROW_NUMBER, but if two people tie at the top, ROW_NUMBER's "row 2" is still a top earner. RANK leaves gaps, so after two people tie at rank 1 the next is rank 3 and a rank of 2 may not even exist. DENSE_RANK ranks distinct values without gaps, so dr = 2 is genuinely the second-highest salary, the answer they want. Knowing WHY, ROW_NUMBER versus RANK versus DENSE_RANK on ties, is the senior signal, and remembering that HAVING filters groups after aggregation while WHERE filters rows before it keeps you out of the second trap.
lessons.example:
How to talk while you code:
Line: "Before I write this, let me confirm the grain, is it one row per order or per order-item? And for the second-highest, should ties count as one value or return multiple people?"
Probe: "Two employees share the second-highest salary, does your query return both? And what if a user has two orders at the exact same timestamp?"
Gotcha: Reaching for ROW_NUMBER on 'second highest' (breaks on ties), forgetting NULLIF so a zero prior month throws divide-by-zero, integer division silently truncating MoM growth to 0, or trying to filter an aggregate in WHERE. Senior signal: stating the grain and tie-handling before the first keystroke.
lessons.code:
-- 1) Latest row per entity: ROW_NUMBER, keep rn = 1
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT o.*,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY created_at DESC) AS rn
  FROM orders o
) t WHERE rn = 1;

-- 2) Second-highest DISTINCT value: DENSE_RANK, keep dr = 2 (handles ties)
SELECT emp_id, salary FROM (
  SELECT emp_id, salary,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dr
  FROM employees
) t WHERE dr = 2;

-- 3) Month-over-month growth: LAG (cast to numeric, guard divide-by-zero)
SELECT month, revenue,
       (revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month)) * 1.0
         / NULLIF(LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month), 0) AS mom_growth
FROM monthly_revenue ORDER BY month;

-- 4) Funnel: conditional aggregation (distinct users reaching each step)
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN step = 'visit'    THEN user_id END)  AS visited,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN step = 'cart'     THEN user_id END)  AS carted,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN step = 'purchase' THEN user_id END)  AS purchased
FROM events;

-- 5) Duplicate detection: GROUP BY ... HAVING
SELECT email, COUNT(*) AS n
FROM users GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
lessons.tryit: On a table orders(order_id, user_id, amount, created_at), write the latest-order-per-user query with ROW_NUMBER. Then write 'second-highest amount' with DENSE_RANK and test it against a deliberate tie (two rows sharing the top amount) to prove it returns the true second value, not another top row.

mistakes.m: Using ROW_NUMBER (or RANK) for 'second highest': ROW_NUMBER breaks on ties and RANK skips ranks so '2' may not exist.
mistakes.fix: Use DENSE_RANK and filter the outer query to = 2; it returns the second-highest DISTINCT value even with ties.

mistakes.m: Computing MoM as (rev - LAG(rev)) / LAG(rev) on integer columns: integer division truncates to 0 and a zero prior month divides by zero.
mistakes.fix: Cast to numeric (multiply by 1.0 or ::numeric) and wrap the denominator in NULLIF(prev, 0).

mistakes.m: Trying to filter an aggregate or window result inside WHERE.
mistakes.fix: Filter aggregates with HAVING, and filter a window column in an outer query or CTE; WHERE runs before grouping and before windows.

links.t: PostgreSQL: Window function reference (row_number, dense_rank, lag)
PostgreSQL: Aggregate functions, GROUP BY and HAVING

quiz.q: You need the second-highest salary, and several employees may tie for the top. Which window function is correct?
opts: ROW_NUMBER()
RANK()
DENSE_RANK()
NTILE(2)
quiz.why: DENSE_RANK ranks distinct values without gaps, so filtering to rank 2 returns the true second-highest value even when many rows tie for first. ROW_NUMBER breaks ties arbitrarily (its 'row 2' can still be a top earner) and RANK leaves gaps so rank 2 may not exist.
quiz.approach: On any 'Nth highest' question, ask about ties first, then reach for DENSE_RANK.

quiz.q: You want the single latest row per user, with all that row's columns. Which approach works?
opts: GROUP BY user_id with MAX(created_at), then select the other columns
ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY created_at DESC), keep rn = 1
ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 1
SELECT DISTINCT user_id
quiz.why: ROW_NUMBER partitioned by user and ordered by time descending numbers each user's rows independently; keeping rn = 1 returns exactly one latest row per user with all its columns. GROUP BY MAX gives the max timestamp but not the rest of that row (and a plain join back to MAX can return two rows on a tie), and LIMIT 1 returns one row for the whole table.
quiz.approach: 'Top-1-per-group' means ROW_NUMBER in a subquery/CTE, filtered to 1.

quiz.q: To return only emails that appear more than once, where does the COUNT(*) > 1 condition belong?
opts: In a WHERE clause
In a HAVING clause
In an ORDER BY clause
In a LIMIT clause
quiz.why: WHERE filters individual rows before grouping, so it cannot see COUNT(*). HAVING filters groups after aggregation, which is exactly where a per-group count exists.
quiz.approach: Aggregate conditions go in HAVING; row conditions go in WHERE.

lessons.title: Power BI, DAX Filter Context, and the Dashboard Case Round
lessons.concept:
Analyst roles in India increasingly mean Power BI, not just SQL, and the concept interviewers use to separate button-clickers from analysts is filter context. Every number in a Power BI visual is computed inside a filter context: the filters coming from the row and column you are on, plus slicers and page filters. A measure like Total Sales = SUM(Sales[Amount]) returns a different number in each cell of a matrix because each cell carries a different context, this Region, this Month. If you can say "a measure has no fixed value; it is evaluated in the filter context of the cell," you have already cleared the beginner bar.

CALCULATE is the one function that MODIFIES that context. Per Microsoft's own docs it "evaluates an expression in a modified filter context": if a column is not already filtered, CALCULATE adds the filter, and if it is, CALCULATE overwrites it. That is how you build a % of grand total (strip filters with REMOVEFILTERS or ALL for the denominator) or a same-period-last-year measure. Know the difference from a calculated column too: a column is computed row by row at refresh and stored, while a measure is computed on the fly and reacts to filters, so "I'd add a column" for a % of total is the junior tell.

The dashboard case round, often a take-home or whiteboard "here is a dataset, design a dashboard," is not graded on how many charts you cram in. It is graded on whether you started from the business question and the audience, picked one primary metric, laid it out with a clear hierarchy (KPIs top-left, then trend, then breakdown), and validated that the numbers tie back to a source. A three-chart dashboard that answers "why did conversion drop?" beats a fifteen-visual wall every time.
lessons.example:
FILTER CONTEXT
Line: "A measure doesn't have one value, it's evaluated in the filter context of wherever it sits. SUM(Sales[Amount]) shows per-region numbers in a matrix because each row supplies a Region filter. For a % of total I use CALCULATE with REMOVEFILTERS to get the denominator that ignores the current cell."
Probe: "So write me '% of grand total'." -> DIVIDE([Total Sales], CALCULATE([Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS(Sales)))
Gotcha: Confusing a measure with a calculated column; saying you'd 'add a column' for a % of total is the junior tell. Senior signal: explaining that CALCULATE overwrites an existing filter on the same column.

DASHBOARD CASE
Line: "Before building I'd ask who reads this and what decision it drives. Then I lead with one primary metric, show its trend, add one or two breakdowns that explain the movement, and reconcile the totals against a known source number before I present."
Probe: "You have 30 possible charts, how do you decide what stays?"
Gotcha: A kitchen-sink dashboard with no hierarchy, or numbers that don't tie out. Senior signal: naming the audience and the one decision, and raising data validation unprompted.
lessons.code:
-- A measure is evaluated in the CURRENT filter context
Total Sales = SUM ( Sales[Amount] )

-- CALCULATE modifies the filter context (forces Color = Blue,
-- overwriting any existing Color filter)
Blue Sales = CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], 'Product'[Color] = "Blue" )

-- % of grand total: strip filters for the denominator so it
-- ignores the current cell's Region/Month
% of Total =
DIVIDE (
    [Total Sales],
    CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS ( Sales ) )
)
lessons.tryit: In Power BI Desktop with any sample sales table, build a matrix of Sales by Region, add a '% of Total' measure using CALCULATE + REMOVEFILTERS, and confirm the regions' percentages sum to 100%. Then write one sentence naming the audience and the single decision your dashboard is meant to support.

mistakes.m: Explaining a measure as though it has one fixed value.
mistakes.fix: State that it is evaluated in the filter context of each cell, so the same measure returns different numbers per row, column, or slicer.

mistakes.m: Using a calculated column where a measure is required, e.g. for a % of total or any filter-responsive aggregate.
mistakes.fix: Use a measure with CALCULATE; columns are static and computed at refresh, measures react to filters.

mistakes.m: Designing the dashboard chart-first instead of question-first.
mistakes.fix: Start from the audience and the one decision, pick a primary metric, then lay out KPIs -> trend -> breakdown and validate the totals.

links.t: Microsoft Learn: DAX overview (incl. filter context)
Microsoft Learn: What is Power BI?

quiz.q: What determines the value a Power BI measure returns?
opts: The filter context of the cell or visual where it is evaluated
It is fixed the moment you create the measure
The physical order of columns in the table
Only the measure's data type
quiz.why: A measure is recomputed on the fly inside the filter context supplied by the row, column, slicers and page filters, so the same measure shows different numbers in different cells. It has no single stored value.
quiz.approach: When asked about measures, anchor on 'evaluated in filter context', never on a stored value.

quiz.q: Which DAX function is designed to modify the filter context?
opts: SUM
COUNTROWS
CALCULATE
RELATED
quiz.why: Microsoft's docs define CALCULATE as evaluating an expression in a modified filter context, adding filters where a column is unfiltered and overwriting filters where one already exists. The others aggregate values or fetch a related value but do not change the surrounding filter context.
quiz.approach: Any '% of total', same-period, or what-if-a-filter question routes through CALCULATE.

quiz.q: For a '% of grand total' number, should you build a measure or a calculated column?
opts: A measure with CALCULATE + REMOVEFILTERS, because it must react to filters
A calculated column, because it is a percentage
Either one works identically
A calculated table
quiz.why: The denominator must ignore the current cell's filters, which only a measure using CALCULATE/REMOVEFILTERS can do dynamically. A calculated column is computed row by row at refresh and cannot react to slicers or cell context.
quiz.approach: If the number must change with slicers or cells, it is a measure, not a column.

lessons.title: Product Sense, Guesstimates, Smart Questions, and Salary Negotiation
lessons.concept:
The 'thinking' round tests judgment, not tools. Product-sense prompts ("how would you improve Swiggy's search?" or "what metric would you track for CRED?") want structure: clarify the user and the goal, pick one north-star metric plus a guardrail, generate two or three ideas, and name a trade-off for each. Guesstimates ("how many two-wheelers are sold in India each year?") want a Fermi approach: state your assumptions aloud, break the number into a population-times-rate chain, use round numbers, and sanity-check the order of magnitude. Interviewers do not care about the exact figure; they grade whether your structure holds and whether you flagged your assumptions.

Then they flip it: "Any questions for us?" This is scored. Silence, or "what does a typical day look like?", is a weak signal. Thoughtful questions about the team's data maturity, how success is measured in the first 90 days, or why the role is open show you already think like an owner. Save compensation for after you have a signal of genuine interest, and usually raise it with the recruiter rather than the technical panel.

Salary negotiation in India runs on a few truths. Your leverage peaks after the offer, not before, because that is when they have chosen you and put budget on the table. The strongest anchor is a researched market range (or a competing offer), and your BATNA, your best alternative such as another offer or staying put, sets your floor. Never volunteer your current CTC as your ceiling; many firms now ask for expected CTC instead, so give a researched range with the top of your target as the anchor. And negotiate the whole package, base, joining bonus, ESOPs, and notice-period buyout, not just the base number.
lessons.example:
PRODUCT SENSE
Line: "Let me clarify the goal and user first, are we optimising for new-user activation or power-user retention? I'll set engagement as the north star, propose two changes, and call out the trade-off each one carries."
Probe: "You proposed X, what metric moves, and what might it quietly hurt?"
Gotcha: Jumping to features ('add dark mode!') before defining the user and goal. Senior signal: naming a guardrail metric, the thing your change might break.

GUESSTIMATE (illustrative, not exact)
Line: "India two-wheeler sales, top-down: ~1.4 billion people, ~250 million households, say 40% own a two-wheeler on an ~8-year replacement cycle, so roughly 100M bikes / 8 = about 12-15 million a year, plus first-time buyers."
Probe: "Which assumption is your answer most sensitive to?"
Gotcha: Silent arithmetic. Say every assumption aloud; a wrong-but-transparent estimate beats a right-but-unexplained one. Senior signal: naming which assumption drives the result.

SMART QUESTIONS
Line: "How will you measure whether this hire is successful in the first 90 days? And how mature is the data stack, am I building foundations or optimising an existing one?"
Gotcha: 'I have no questions', or only asking about leave policy. Senior signal: questions that show you're already thinking about impact.

SALARY
Line: "Based on my research for this role and location I'm targeting X-Y, and given [specific strength] I'd be at the upper end. Is there flexibility on base, and how do ESOPs and joining bonus factor in?"
Probe: "What's your current CTC?" -> deflect: "I'd rather anchor on the value for this role; my expectation is X-Y."
Gotcha: Blurting current CTC as your number, or negotiating before you hold the offer.
lessons.code:
# Product sense (CIRCLES-lite)
1. Clarify   user + goal (activation? retention? revenue?)
2. Metric    pick ONE north star + a guardrail metric
3. Ideas     2-3 changes, prioritised
4. Trade-off name what each idea might hurt

# Guesstimate (Fermi): decompose, assume OUT LOUD
Answer  = Population x Rate x Frequency
Example = Households x ownership% x (1 / replacement_years)
-> round numbers, then sanity-check the order of magnitude

# Negotiation
Leverage = AFTER the offer, not before
Anchor   = researched range (top of target) OR competing offer
BATNA    = your best alternative = your floor
Scope    = base + joining bonus + ESOPs + notice buyout
lessons.tryit: Pick a product you use daily (PhonePe, Zomato, or IRCTC) and give a 3-minute answer to 'how would you improve it?' using Clarify -> Metric -> Ideas -> Trade-off. Then research the market range for your target role on one salary site and write your two-line negotiation anchor: the range, and why you sit at the top of it.

mistakes.m: Jumping to feature ideas in a product question before defining the user and success metric.
mistakes.fix: Clarify the user and goal and name a north-star metric first, then propose ideas each with a trade-off and a guardrail.

mistakes.m: Doing guesstimate arithmetic silently, or chasing false precision.
mistakes.fix: State every assumption aloud, use round numbers, and sanity-check the order of magnitude; structure is what's graded, not the exact figure.

mistakes.m: Revealing your current or expected CTC as a hard number before you have leverage.
mistakes.fix: Wait for the offer, anchor on a researched range with the top as your target, and negotiate the whole package, not just base.

links.t: BATNA (best alternative to a negotiated agreement)

quiz.q: What should you do first in a 'how would you improve this product?' question?
opts: Clarify the target user and the goal or metric
List as many features as you can think of
Describe the technical architecture
Give your gut opinion immediately
quiz.why: Interviewers grade structure. Defining who the user is and what success metric you're optimising frames every idea that follows and lets you prioritise and defend them; ideas without a goal are just random feature-listing.
quiz.approach: Clarify user + goal + metric before proposing any idea.

quiz.q: In a guesstimate, what is actually being graded?
opts: Hitting the exact real-world number
Your assumptions, structure, and sanity-check, spoken aloud
Doing the arithmetic silently in your head
Reciting memorised statistics
quiz.why: Guesstimates test reasoning under uncertainty. A transparent chain of stated assumptions with an order-of-magnitude sanity check is the signal, not precision, since the true figure is unknown to both of you anyway.
quiz.approach: Decompose, assume out loud, round, then sanity-check the magnitude.

quiz.q: When do you have the most leverage to negotiate salary?
opts: In the first HR screening call
Before you apply
After you receive the offer
During the technical round
quiz.why: Leverage peaks once they've chosen you and issued an offer: they've invested in the process and have budget on the table. Discussing numbers earlier lets them anchor low or screen you out on cost before they're committed to you.
quiz.approach: Get the offer first, then anchor on a researched range and negotiate the full package.

lessons.title: Your 90-Second Story, STAR & the AI-Honesty Question
lessons.concept:
The first two minutes decide the room. Almost every Indian data/analyst interview opens with "tell me about yourself" (TMAY) — treat it as a 90-second trailer, not a read-aloud of your CV. The structure that works is present → past → future: one line on who you are now, one recent win with a number attached, one line on why this role. INTERVIEW LINE (what to actually say): "I'm a Business Analyst at Cars24 working in central analytics on post-sale services. Most recently I replaced 10–15 fragmented Google Sheets with a single Power BI dashboard tracking 77,000+ monthly insurance leads on a Snowflake layer, and caught a lead-attribution bug before launch. I'm now looking for a product-analyst role where that funnel-and-BI work is the core, not a side project." THE PROBE: "Walk me through that bug." Have the one-liner ready.

Behavioural rounds recycle four story archetypes — a win, a failure, a conflict, a deadline — and STAR (Situation, Task, Action, Result) is how you keep them tight. Spend roughly 70% of each story on Action and Result; recruiters in India especially probe failure and conflict to test whether you own outcomes or hand out blame. THE PROBE after any failure story is "what would you do differently?" — so build the lesson into the Result, not as an afterthought. GOTCHA / SENIOR SIGNAL: a candidate who says "the project got deprioritised" fails; one who says "I built on a data model I hadn't validated — now I check the foundation first" passes, because self-awareness is the thing being scored.

The newest question in the room is the AI one: "Do you use ChatGPT/Claude? Did you write this yourself?" Calibrated honesty is the senior move — own the logic, name AI as a validated accelerator. INTERVIEW LINE: "I understand the logic and filter context; for complex DAX measures I use AI tools to accelerate the syntax, then validate the output against source data." GOTCHA: hiding AI use collapses on the first whiteboard follow-up, and claiming solo expert-level depth collapses just as fast — both read as insecurity. Naming it openly, while showing you own correctness, is the differentiator.
lessons.example: A weak TMAY: "I did B.Com, then a PGDM in finance and marketing, then I joined Ceasefire in sales, then Wheelseye as an analyst, then Ozone, and now Cars24 since 2023..." — thirty seconds in, the panel has stopped listening. The strong version skips the timeline, opens on the present, and leads with one quantified, role-relevant win. Same career, completely different first impression.
lessons.code:
TMAY — 90-second skeleton (present → past → future)
[NOW]    Role + where you sit + what you own (1 line)
[PROOF]  One recent win WITH a number (1–2 lines)
[WHY]    Why THIS role / company, honestly (1 line)
-> Stop. Leave a hook for the first follow-up.

STAR — map your 4 archetypes to real material
WIN      Insure24: 10–15 sheets -> 1 dashboard, 77K leads, bug caught pre-launch
FAILURE  Challan AI agent didn't ship -> lesson: validate the data model first
CONFLICT Metric-definition disagreement -> proved the double-count with source data
DEADLINE Pre-launch DAX bug fixes under a go-live date -> validated, then shipped
(Use a TRUE instance for conflict/deadline; STAR = Situation, Task, Action[~40%], Result[~30% + lesson])

AI-HONESTY line (memorise word-for-word)
"I own the logic and filter context; AI accelerates complex syntax, and I
 validate every output against source data before it ships."
PROBE: "So could you write it without AI?" -> explain the approach aloud
 (context transition, CALCULATE + REMOVEFILTERS). You own correctness; AI owns speed.
lessons.tryit: Record yourself on your phone delivering (a) a 90-second TMAY and (b) one STAR failure story. Time both. If TMAY runs past 95 seconds or the failure story spends more than 20% on the Situation, cut and re-record until it's tight.

mistakes.m: Reciting your CV chronologically in TMAY — panel disengages by the 30-second mark.
mistakes.fix: Use present → past → future. Open on your current role, lead with one quantified recent win, and stop at 90 seconds with a hook.

mistakes.m: In a failure story, blaming the org, a teammate, or 'shifting priorities'.
mistakes.fix: Own your slice of it and spend most of your words on the corrective lesson and what you now do differently.

mistakes.m: Hiding AI use, or claiming independent expert-level SQL/DAX depth you can't defend on a whiteboard.
mistakes.fix: State it plainly: 'I own the logic and validate against source; AI accelerates syntax.' It survives the next question — overclaiming doesn't.

quiz.q: In a 90-second 'tell me about yourself', what should the largest share of time go to?
opts: A chronological list of every job you've held since college
A recent, relevant achievement with a quantified result tied to the role you're interviewing for
Your hobbies and personal background to build rapport
The reasons you're unhappy in your current job
quiz.why: The pitch is a trailer, not the full film — lead with present relevance and a measurable win (e.g., '10–15 sheets into one dashboard tracking 77K leads'), because interviewers decide in the first two minutes whether to lean in. Chronology bores them; complaints signal risk.
quiz.approach: Present → past (one proof point) → future. Rehearse to 90 seconds and end on a hook that invites the first question.

quiz.q: In a STAR 'failure' story, which element most determines whether you pass?
opts: The Situation — described in maximum detail
The Task — framed to prove it was someone else's fault
The Result — what you learned and changed, stated honestly
Skipping the failure and redirecting to a success
quiz.why: Interviewers ask for failure to test self-awareness and growth, not to trap you. A true miss with a concrete lesson (the Challan agent that didn't ship → 'validate the data foundation before building on it') lands far better than a humble-brag or a dodge, which reads as evasive.
quiz.approach: Pick a real, low-blast-radius failure you owned; spend most of your words on the corrective action and what you now do differently.

quiz.q: You use Claude/ChatGPT daily for complex DAX. Asked 'did you write this yourself?', the strongest answer is:
opts: 'Yes, entirely on my own — I never use AI tools'
'I understand the logic and filter context; I use AI to accelerate complex syntax, then validate the output against source data'
'AI wrote all of it, so I can't really explain the logic'
'I'd rather not say how I built it'
quiz.why: Calibrated honesty wins because the claim survives the next question. Owning the logic (context transition, CALCULATE) while naming AI as a validated accelerator is both true and senior; claiming pure independent expertise collapses on the first whiteboard follow-up, and disclaiming all understanding fails the role.
quiz.approach: Frame AI as an accelerator you supervise — you own correctness by validating against source. Never hide it, never inflate past it.

lessons.title: The 5 SQL Patterns Interviewers Reuse
lessons.concept:
Data-analyst SQL rounds in India — product firms, the fintech/NBFC lending shops around Gurgaon, e-commerce — recycle a surprisingly small set of problems. Master five and you cover most technical screens: latest-row-per-entity (ROW_NUMBER), second-highest (DENSE_RANK), month-over-month (LAG), funnel (conditional aggregation), and duplicate detection (GROUP BY … HAVING). Four of the five are window functions, so the fastest way to signal competence is to name them by function and explain why a window beats a plain GROUP BY.

The through-line: "top-N-per-group," "Nth-highest value," and "compare to the previous row" are window-function problems, not GROUP BY problems. THE PROBE the interviewer always adds is "why not MAX or GROUP BY?" — and the answer is that GROUP BY collapses rows and throws away every other column, whereas a window function keeps the full row and just attaches a ranking or a lagged value beside it. Say that sentence out loud and you've passed the concept even before you finish typing.

The gotchas separate juniors from seniors. DENSE_RANK vs RANK vs ROW_NUMBER matters the instant there's a tie: with two rows tied at the top, RANK gives 1, 1, 3 (it leaves a gap), ROW_NUMBER gives 1, 2 (it breaks the tie arbitrarily), and DENSE_RANK gives 1, 1, 2 (no gap) — so only DENSE_RANK's rank 2 is the true second distinct value. LAG returns the previous ROW, not the previous calendar month, so a missing month silently corrupts your MoM %. A funnel built with conditional aggregation must COUNT(DISTINCT user) and respect stage order, or you get the absurd result of more purchases than carts. And HAVING filters groups after aggregation while WHERE filters rows before it — mix them up and the query either errors or lies.
lessons.example: The funnel gotcha is not academic. In the Insure24 dashboard, an early version counted a later funnel stage without enforcing that the earlier stage happened first, adding thousands of invalid 'leads' and letting a downstream stage outnumber the one above it. The fix was exactly the senior move here: count distinct users per stage and only credit a stage when the prior stage actually occurred — monotonic funnel, believable numbers.
lessons.code:
-- 1) LATEST ROW PER ENTITY  (top-N-per-group)
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT o.*,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC) AS rn
  FROM orders o
) t WHERE rn = 1;                       -- PROBE: why not MAX(order_date)? -> MAX loses the other columns

-- 2) SECOND-HIGHEST DISTINCT SALARY
SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM (
  SELECT salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dr
  FROM employees
) t WHERE dr = 2;                       -- GOTCHA: ties at the top -> RANK skips 2, ROW_NUMBER picks a duplicate

-- 3) MONTH-OVER-MONTH %
SELECT month, revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev,
  ROUND(100.0*(revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month))
        / NULLIF(LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month),0), 1) AS mom_pct
FROM monthly_revenue;                   -- GOTCHA: LAG = previous ROW, not previous month; NULLIF avoids /0

-- 4) FUNNEL via conditional aggregation
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN stage='view'     THEN user_id END) AS viewed,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN stage='add_cart' THEN user_id END) AS carted,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN stage='purchase' THEN user_id END) AS purchased
FROM events;                            -- SENIOR SIGNAL: DISTINCT users + enforce stage order (no purchase > cart)

-- 5) DUPLICATE DETECTION
SELECT email, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM users GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;   -- HAVING filters groups; WHERE filters rows
lessons.tryit: On any sample orders table, write all five queries from memory. Then deliberately break #3: delete every row for one month and re-run the MoM query — watch the % for the following month compare against two-months-ago and produce a wrong number. That's the LAG trap, felt once, remembered forever.

mistakes.m: Using GROUP BY + MAX(date) for 'the latest row' and then losing every other column from that row.
mistakes.fix: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY entity ORDER BY ts DESC) and filter rn = 1 — the full row survives intact.

mistakes.m: Using RANK or ROW_NUMBER for 'second-highest distinct salary' when there's a tie at the top.
mistakes.fix: DENSE_RANK gives no gaps and no duplicates, so dr = 2 is the true second distinct value. Clarify whether they mean distinct value or Nth row.

mistakes.m: A funnel where 'purchased' exceeds 'carted' because you counted events, not users, and ignored stage order.
mistakes.fix: COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) per stage and only credit a stage when the prior stage occurred, keeping the funnel monotonic.

links.t: PostgreSQL — Window function list (RANK, DENSE_RANK, LAG)

quiz.q: To return the single most recent order row (all its columns) per customer, the cleanest pattern is:
opts: SELECT customer_id, MAX(order_date) FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id
ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date DESC), then keep rn = 1
SELECT DISTINCT customer_id FROM orders
SELECT * FROM orders ORDER BY order_date DESC LIMIT 1
quiz.why: MAX(order_date) returns only the date, not the other columns of that row, and needs a self-join to recover them; LIMIT 1 returns one row for the whole table, not one per customer. ROW_NUMBER partitioned by customer and ordered by date descending tags the latest row per customer as rn = 1 with every column intact.
quiz.approach: 'Top-N-per-group' almost always means a window function. Add a tie-breaker column to ORDER BY so ties resolve deterministically.

quiz.q: Two employees share the highest salary. To get the second-highest DISTINCT salary, which is safe?
opts: ROW_NUMBER() ... WHERE rn = 2
RANK() ... WHERE rank = 2
DENSE_RANK() ... WHERE dense_rank = 2
SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees
quiz.why: With a tie at the top, ROW_NUMBER labels the two top earners 1 and 2, so rn = 2 returns another top-salary row (wrong). RANK skips to 3 after the tie (1,1,3), so rank = 2 returns nothing. DENSE_RANK produces no gaps (1,1,2), so the second distinct salary is correctly at rank 2.
quiz.approach: For 'Nth highest distinct value' use DENSE_RANK. If they want the Nth row regardless of ties, use ROW_NUMBER or LIMIT/OFFSET — clarify which they mean.

quiz.q: Computing month-over-month % with LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month), what's the classic trap?
opts: LAG can only look forward, never backward
If a calendar month has no row, LAG uses the previous existing row — not the true prior month — skewing the change
LAG requires a PARTITION BY clause or it errors
You must always divide by the current month's revenue
quiz.why: LAG returns the previous row in the ordered set, not the previous calendar period. A gap month makes LAG compare against two-months-ago, silently corrupting the %; the fix is a complete, gap-free date spine left-joined to the data. Also wrap the denominator in NULLIF to avoid divide-by-zero.
quiz.approach: State your grain and completeness assumptions out loud. For time-series deltas, guarantee a gap-free calendar before trusting LAG/LEAD.

lessons.title: DAX Filter Context & the Dashboard Case Round
lessons.concept:
Power BI rounds test one idea above all others: filter context. A measure is evaluated inside the filters flowing from slicers, the visual's own rows and columns, and the filter pane — change those filters and the same measure returns a different number. Row context is a different thing entirely: it's the notion of a "current row" during iteration, which is what a calculated column or an iterator like SUMX sees. CALCULATE is the single most important DAX function because it is essentially the only function that modifies filter context — its table-returning sibling CALCULATETABLE does the same for tables — and it also triggers context transition, turning the current row context into an equivalent filter context. If you can say that cleanly, you are ahead of most candidates in the room.

The probes are predictable. "Why does my % of total read 100% on every row?" — because you didn't lift the row's own filter off the denominator; wrap it in CALCULATE with REMOVEFILTERS (or ALL). "Why does SUM in a calculated column return the same total on every row?" — because a calculated column has row context but no filter context, so the SUM sees the whole column, not the current row; aggregations belong in measures. Prefer DIVIDE over the "/" operator so a zero denominator returns BLANK instead of an error. And TREATAS lets you apply a disconnected table as a filter across a missing relationship — the exact trick used for UTM attribution in the Insure24 build.

The dashboard case round is a design conversation disguised as a whiteboard task: "design a dashboard for the head of sales." The senior sequence is decision → metric → layout → grain/refresh → guardrail. INTERVIEW LINE to open with: "Before I pick any visuals — who is the primary user, and what recurring decision does this dashboard need to drive?" GOTCHA: candidates who leap straight to chart types and colours, or who cram 30 vanity KPIs onto one page, signal no product thinking. SENIOR SIGNAL: naming a single top-left North Star, then a counter/guardrail metric beside it, and stating the refresh cadence the decision actually needs.
lessons.example: Insure24 is the case round made real: 10–15 Google Sheets collapsed into one Power BI dashboard on a Snowflake layer, with a 7-stage funnel whose North Star (Transaction Success) sits top-left, MTD-vs-LMTD comparisons built with time-intelligence (DATEADD over a proper date table) and % of total via REMOVEFILTERS, and UTM attribution driven through disconnected dimension tables via TREATAS. Decision first, visuals last.
lessons.code:
-- FILTER CONTEXT in three measures --------------------------------
Total Sales   = SUM ( Sales[Amount] )

% Grand Total =                       -- fixes the '100% on every row' bug
  DIVIDE (
    [Total Sales],
    CALCULATE ( [Total Sales], REMOVEFILTERS ( Sales ) )  -- lift the row's own filter
  )

MTD Sales     = TOTALMTD ( [Total Sales], 'Calendar'[Date] )

-- WHY a calculated column misbehaves:
--   =SUM(Sales[Amount]) in a column -> row context, NO filter context -> whole-column total
--   Use the column directly (Sales[Amount]) or RELATED(); put aggregations in MEASURES.
-- CALCULATE = the function that modifies filter context (+ context transition).

-- DASHBOARD CASE — the senior sequence ----------------------------
1 DECISION  "Who's the user? What weekly decision does this drive?"
2 METRIC    One North Star top-left + a guardrail/counter-metric
3 LAYOUT    KPIs -> trend -> breakdown -> drill-through
4 GRAIN     Data grain + refresh cadence the decision actually needs
5 GUARDRAIL Name the counter-metric out loud   <- senior signal
lessons.tryit: In Power BI, build a tiny two-measure report: Total Sales and % Grand Total. Confirm the % column actually sums to 100% down the table (if it shows 100% on every row, you forgot REMOVEFILTERS). Then, in one sentence, write the opening question you'd ask to start any dashboard case round.

mistakes.m: Explaining CALCULATE as 'it filters the data' — vague, and it's the exact thing being tested.
mistakes.fix: Say it precisely: 'CALCULATE modifies the filter context and triggers context transition.' That phrasing is the senior tell.

mistakes.m: Using the '/' operator for ratios and hitting divide-by-zero errors or blanks in production.
mistakes.fix: Use DIVIDE(numerator, denominator[, alternate]) — it returns BLANK (or your fallback) safely on a zero denominator.

mistakes.m: Starting a dashboard case by choosing chart types and a colour theme.
mistakes.fix: Lead with user + decision + the single top metric, and name a guardrail metric before you draw a single visual.

quiz.q: What does CALCULATE do that makes it the most important DAX function?
opts: It is the only function that can sort a visual
It modifies the filter context in which its expression is evaluated (and triggers context transition)
It converts a measure into a calculated column
It always removes every filter from the model
quiz.why: CALCULATE evaluates its filter arguments and applies them to the filter context before computing its expression — it is the one function that can add, replace, or remove filters, and it turns row context into filter context. ALL/REMOVEFILTERS only remove filters; they don't do everything CALCULATE does.
quiz.approach: When a measure 'won't respect' or 'won't ignore' a filter, reach for CALCULATE with the right modifier (KEEPFILTERS, REMOVEFILTERS, ALLEXCEPT).

quiz.q: In a calculated column, '=SUM(Sales[Amount])' returns the same total on every row. Why?
opts: A calculated column has row context but no filter context, so SUM aggregates the entire column
SUM is not permitted inside calculated columns
The table relationship is inactive
You must use DIVIDE instead of SUM
quiz.why: A calculated column is evaluated row by row (row context) but carries no filter context, so an aggregation like SUM sees the whole column, not the current row. To reference the current row, use the column directly or RELATED; put aggregations in measures, where filter context comes from the visual.
quiz.approach: Row context ≠ filter context. Prefer measures over calculated columns for anything aggregated — they respond to slicers and cost far less memory.

quiz.q: You're asked to design a dashboard for the head of sales. The strongest first move is:
opts: Immediately pick chart types and a colour theme
Ask who the primary user is and what recurring decision the dashboard must drive, then define the top metric
Put all 30 available KPIs on one page so nothing is missed
Decide the data refresh frequency before anything else
quiz.why: A dashboard is a decision tool, not a chart gallery. Clarifying the user and the weekly decision fixes the single top-left metric and what to leave out; jumping to visuals or dumping 30 KPIs signals no product thinking. Refresh cadence matters, but it follows from the decision, not before it.
quiz.approach: Decision → metric → layout (KPIs → trend → breakdown → drill) → grain/refresh → one guardrail metric. Naming the counter-metric is the senior signal.

lessons.title: Product Sense, Guesstimates, Smart Questions & Negotiation
lessons.concept:
The judgment rounds reward method over trivia. Guesstimates — "how many cars does Cars24 sell in Delhi a month?", "food-delivery orders per day in Bengaluru?" — test whether you can reason under uncertainty, not whether you memorised a figure. The template is population → segments → rate → round arithmetic → sanity check, with every assumption spoken aloud so the interviewer can correct an anchor and follow your logic. The sanity check is not a formality: cross-check your answer against a number you already know, and if it looks off, say so and revisit the shakiest assumption — catching your own over-estimate scores higher than a confident wrong number. Product sense layers on top: pick a metric to move, state a hypothesis, and propose how you'd measure it — an A/B test with a clearly named guardrail metric so a win on one number doesn't quietly break another.

"Do you have any questions for us?" is a scored question, not a closing courtesy. Senior-signal questions probe how the work actually happens: what does success in this role look like at six months; is the analytics function centralised or embedded in product teams; what's the data stack and the biggest data-quality pain right now; why is this role open. GOTCHA: answering "no, you've covered everything" reads as disengaged, and leading with leave policy, WFH, or promotion timelines in the first conversation signals the wrong priorities — save logistics for the recruiter and offer stage.

Salary negotiation in India has its own choreography: recruiters ask your current CTC and expected CTC early, often on the first call. The trap is anchoring your expectation to a small percentage bump on your current salary. Instead, anchor on the role and the market, give a researched range with your target near its floor, and negotiate the whole structure — fixed, variable, ESOP, joining bonus, notice-period buyout — not a single number. INTERVIEW LINE for expected CTC: "Based on the scope of this role and the market, I'm looking in the range of 16–18 LPA, and I'm open to discussing the complete structure." GOTCHA: blurting your current CTC first often boxes you into a 10% bump; stay collaborative, and know your walk-away (BATNA) before you pick up the phone.
lessons.example: A guesstimate for 'food-delivery orders per day in Bengaluru': metro population ~13M; the order-capable segment (urban, smartphone, disposable income) ~30% → ~4M; assume an average of 1 order every 4 days → 0.25/person/day; so ~4M × 0.25 ≈ 1M orders/day. Now sanity-check it: Swiggy and Zomato together do only a few million orders a day across all of India, so a single metro at ~1M would be roughly a quarter-to-a-third of the whole country — too high. That flags the weakest anchor: '1 order per 4 days' is optimistic. Dial it to ~1 per 8–10 days and you land nearer 0.3–0.5M/day, a far more defensible figure. The exact number is secondary; the visible method — and catching your own over-estimate — is what's being scored.
lessons.code:
GUESSTIMATE — orders/day, Bengaluru (say every assumption aloud)
  Population (metro)         ~13,000,000
  x Order-capable segment    ~30%          -> ~4,000,000
  x Frequency (1 per 4 days)  0.25/day      -> ~1,000,000 orders/day
  SANITY CHECK: ~1M is ~1/4-1/3 of all-India volume -> too high;
                revisit frequency (~1 per 8-10 days) -> ~0.3-0.5M/day
PROBE: "Your number looks high" -> agree, point to the frequency anchor, re-estimate

SMART QUESTIONS (pick 3–4; senior signal)
  - What does success in this role look like at 6 months?
  - Is analytics centralised or embedded in product teams?
  - What's the data stack, and the biggest data-quality pain today?
  - Why is this role open?
GOTCHA: "No questions" / leading with leave & WFH -> save logistics for recruiter stage

NEGOTIATION — expected CTC
  "Based on the scope and the market, I'm looking at 16–18 LPA,
   and I'm open to discussing the complete structure."
  PROBE: "That's above our band — can you be flexible?"
   -> "What's the band? Let's look at fixed vs variable, ESOP, joining bonus."
  GOTCHA: revealing current CTC first -> anchored to a ~10% bump. Know your walk-away (BATNA).
lessons.tryit: Do one guesstimate end-to-end on paper for two-wheelers in your own city — write the assumptions, the arithmetic, and an explicit sanity check that actually cross-references a number you know. Then write out your three closing questions and a single-sentence expected-CTC range you could say on a recruiter call without hesitating.

mistakes.m: Guessing a precise number fast, with no stated assumptions and no sanity check.
mistakes.fix: Use round numbers, segment the population, and cross-check the final magnitude against something you already know. Method scores, not precision.

mistakes.m: Sanity-checking as a formality — noticing the number looks off but defending it anyway.
mistakes.fix: When the cross-check fails, say so and revisit the weakest anchor out loud. Correcting yourself signals judgment; doubling down signals the opposite.

mistakes.m: Answering 'no, I'm good' when asked if you have questions, or volunteering your current CTC and expecting a raise on top of it.
mistakes.fix: Ask 3–4 prepared questions about success metrics, team, and stack. For pay, anchor to market value with a range and negotiate the total structure — hold your walk-away number.

links.t: BATNA — best alternative to a negotiated agreement (Wikipedia)

quiz.q: In a guesstimate like 'food-delivery orders per day in Bengaluru', what matters most to the interviewer?
opts: Arriving at the exact official figure
A clear, segmented method with stated assumptions and a sanity check that you act on
Answering in under ten seconds
Quoting a statistic you memorised
quiz.why: Guesstimates test structured reasoning under uncertainty, not recall. Stating anchors (~13M people, ~30% order-capable, a frequency rate), doing round arithmetic, then cross-checking the result against a known number — and revisiting an anchor if it looks off — shows how you'd size a market or a feature. The final number only needs the right order of magnitude.
quiz.approach: Population → segments → rate → arithmetic → sanity check. Say assumptions aloud, and if the cross-check fails, correct the weakest anchor rather than defending the number.

quiz.q: When a round ends with 'any questions for us?', which choice signals seniority?
opts: 'No, I think you've covered everything'
'What does success in this role look like at six months, and how is the analytics function structured?'
'What's the leave policy and the work-from-home rule?'
'How soon can I expect a promotion?'
quiz.why: Questions about success criteria, team structure, and data maturity show you're evaluating impact and fit, not perks. 'No questions' reads as disengaged, and leading with leave or promotion signals the wrong priorities in a first conversation — those belong at the recruiter or offer stage.
quiz.approach: Prepare 3–4 questions about the decision the role supports, the data stack, and how success is measured. Save comp and policy for later.

quiz.q: A recruiter asks your expected CTC. Current is ~11.5 LPA and you want a real step-up. Strongest reply:
opts: 'Whatever you think is fair is fine with me'
'Exactly ₹11.5 LPA — same as now'
'Based on the role's scope and the market, I'm looking at ₹16–18 LPA, open to discussing the full structure'
'I won't share a number until I see the offer'
quiz.why: Anchoring on the role and market (not your current salary) with a researched range keeps you from being boxed into a small percentage bump, while 'open to the full structure' stays collaborative and leaves room on fixed/variable/joining bonus. Deflecting entirely or under-anchoring both weaken your position.
quiz.approach: Anchor to market value, give a range with your target near its floor, and negotiate total structure — not one number. Know your walk-away (BATNA).

lessons.title: The 90-Second Pitch, STAR Stories & the AI Question
lessons.concept:
In an Indian data-analyst interview, "tell me about yourself" is not an invitation to recite your CV from Class 10 marks onward — it is a 90-second, present-past-future pitch. Interview line: "I'm a data analyst at X where I own the reporting for Y, and last quarter I found the drop-off on the OTP step and lifted conversion 12%." Then one line of past (how you got here) and one of future (why THIS role). The probe is always "tell me more about that 12% — what did you actually do?", so every claim must survive two follow-ups. Gotcha / senior signal: candidates who narrate chronologically ("I did B.Com, then...") lose the room; leading with a quantified, relevant win under two minutes is the senior tell.

Structure every behavioural answer as STAR — Situation, Task, Action, Result — and keep the Action in first person ("I built", not "we built"). Have four stories loaded: a win, a failure, a conflict, and a deadline. For failure, pick a genuine miss (a leadership-review number that was wrong because you didn't validate a join) and end on what you changed. The probe on a win is "what was YOUR specific contribution?"; on a failure, "what would you do differently?". Gotcha / senior signal: the humblebrag failure ("I'm too much of a perfectionist") reads as low self-awareness and quietly fails you — an honest, quantified miss with a clear personal role is what impresses.

By 2026 interviewers openly ask, "do you use ChatGPT or Copilot for your SQL and dashboards?" — and both extreme answers lose. Interview line: "Yes, I use it to draft boilerplate and unblock syntax, but I verify every query against the data, understand the logic, and can reproduce it without help." The probe is that they then make you write or explain a query live, so your claim must be true. Gotcha / senior signal: claiming you never use AI is not credible, and over-relying so you cannot explain your own output is disqualifying — owning the tool while owning the correctness is the calibrated, senior answer.
lessons.example: Two candidates report the same 12% uplift. Priya says, "we improved conversion." Rahul says, "I found the drop-off was on the OTP step, A/B tested a resend button, and conversion rose 12% over three weeks." Same result — but Rahul's Action and Result are specific and personal, so Rahul gets the callback.
lessons.code:
TMAY (90s) = Present  (current role + 1 quantified win)
           + Past     (1 line: how I got here)
           + Future   (why THIS role / company)

STAR = Situation (1 line of context)
     + Task      (what was mine to solve)
     + Action    ("I ..." — 2-3 concrete steps)
     + Result    (a number + what it changed)
lessons.tryit: Write your 90-second pitch as exactly three sentences (present, past, future) and record yourself — if it runs past 100 seconds or contains no number, cut and rewrite. Then draft your four STAR stories (win, failure, conflict, deadline) on one index card each, with the Action written in first person.

mistakes.m: Reciting your CV chronologically from education onward
mistakes.fix: Lead with your current role and one quantified, role-relevant win, then use the present-past-future arc.

mistakes.m: Picking a fake 'perfectionist' failure
mistakes.fix: Choose a real miss, own your specific part, and close with the concrete change you made afterwards.

mistakes.m: Claiming you never touch AI tools
mistakes.fix: Say you use them to accelerate but verify every line and can explain it — then be ready to prove it in the live round.

quiz.q: In a 90-second 'tell me about yourself' for a data-analyst role, what should lead?
opts: Your Class 10/12 marks and college timeline in order
Your current role plus one quantified, role-relevant win (present-past-future)
Your hobbies, hometown and family background
An exhaustive list of every tool you have ever touched
quiz.why: Present-past-future keeps it a tight pitch focused on relevance and impact, not a chronological CV readout the interviewer can already see on paper. Leading with a number gives the interviewer something concrete to probe, which is exactly what you want to steer toward.
quiz.approach: Open with who you are now plus one number, one line of past, then why this role.

quiz.q: What most often sinks a STAR 'failure' answer?
opts: Choosing a disguised humblebrag like 'I'm too much of a perfectionist'
Quantifying the result of what went wrong
Keeping the Situation to a single line
Saying 'I' instead of 'we' when describing the Action
quiz.why: A fake failure signals low self-awareness and looks rehearsed; interviewers want a real miss, your specific role in it, and the concrete change you made after. Quantifying, a short Situation, and first-person 'I' are all things you SHOULD do — none of them sink the answer.
quiz.approach: Pick a genuine failure, own your Action, and close on the lesson and what you now do differently.

quiz.q: Asked 'do you use ChatGPT/Copilot for your SQL?', the strongest answer is:
opts: 'No, never — I write everything from scratch.'
'Yes, it writes all my queries, so I'm very fast.'
'Yes — I use it to draft and unblock syntax, but I verify every query against the data and can explain each line.'
'I'd rather not say.'
quiz.why: Denial is not credible in 2026 and total reliance is disqualifying the moment they ask you to explain a query live; owning the tool while owning correctness is the calibrated answer — and you must actually be able to prove it in the room.
quiz.approach: Use AI to accelerate, verify everything, and be ready to write or explain the query without it.

lessons.title: The Five SQL Patterns Interviewers Reuse
lessons.concept:
Most data-analyst SQL rounds — at Flipkart, Swiggy, PhonePe or any service firm — reuse the same five shapes, so master these and almost any question becomes a variant you recognise. (1) Latest row per entity: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY entity ORDER BY time DESC), keep rn = 1. Interview line while coding: "I'll number each user's rows by recency and keep the first." The probe: "what if two events share the exact same timestamp?" — add a tie-breaker column (e.g. event_id DESC) to the ORDER BY so the result is deterministic. Gotcha / senior signal: a GROUP BY with MAX(time) returns the max time but cannot pull the other columns of that row without a self-join back to the table, so the window version is cleaner — and knowing why is the senior tell.

(2) Second-highest via DENSE_RANK, (3) MoM via LAG. For second-highest salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) then filter rank = 2 — because with ties at the top, ROW_NUMBER would label those duplicates 1, 2, 3 and 'rank 2' would just return the top value again. The probe: "what about the Nth highest, or per department?" — parametrise the rank and add PARTITION BY department. For month-on-month growth, LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) gives the previous row; the first month is NULL (no prior row) and any percentage using it is NULL, not zero — a favourite trap. Gotcha / senior signal: remembering the LAG edge is NULL and guarding the divisor with NULLIF so you never divide by zero.

(4) Funnel via conditional aggregation, (5) duplicates via GROUP BY ... HAVING. A funnel (view to cart to purchase) is one pass — COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN step = 'view' THEN user_id END) per stage, no self-joins. The probe: "conversion between stages?" — divide adjacent stages while guarding divide-by-zero with NULLIF. Duplicate detection is GROUP BY the key columns and HAVING COUNT(*) > 1. Gotcha / senior signal: a "duplicate" is defined by a chosen set of columns (email, or name + dob), and HAVING filters groups after aggregation while WHERE filters rows before it — so an aggregate like COUNT(*) cannot sit in WHERE.
lessons.example: PhonePe asks: "from this transactions table, get each user's most recent successful payment." It is just pattern 1 with a WHERE status = 'SUCCESS' before the window, ORDER BY txn_time DESC, keep rn = 1. Recognising the pattern is 80% of the answer.
lessons.code:
-- 1) Latest row per entity (keeps all columns)
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT e.*,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id
                            ORDER BY event_time DESC, event_id DESC) AS rn
  FROM events e
) t
WHERE rn = 1;

-- 2) Second-highest DISTINCT value, ties-safe
SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM (
  SELECT salary,
         DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dr
  FROM employees
) t
WHERE dr = 2;

-- 3) Month-on-month growth %
SELECT month, revenue,
       LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_rev,
       ROUND( (revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month)) * 100.0
              / NULLIF(LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month), 0), 2) AS mom_pct
FROM monthly_revenue;

-- 4) Funnel in one pass (conditional aggregation)
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN step='view'     THEN user_id END) AS viewed,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN step='cart'     THEN user_id END) AS carted,
  COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN step='purchase' THEN user_id END) AS purchased
FROM events;

-- 5) Duplicate detection
SELECT email, COUNT(*) AS cnt
FROM users
GROUP BY email
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
lessons.tryit: On any table you have (or a small mock one), write all five queries from memory in under 15 minutes. Then break query 1: insert two rows with an identical timestamp for one user, watch the tie produce a non-deterministic result, and fix it by adding a second column (like event_id DESC) to the ORDER BY.

mistakes.m: Using ROW_NUMBER for 'second highest' when top values can tie
mistakes.fix: Use DENSE_RANK so tied top values share rank 1 and rank 2 is the true second distinct value.

mistakes.m: Reporting the first month's MoM% as 0
mistakes.fix: LAG returns NULL at the series edge; keep it NULL or COALESCE it deliberately, and guard the divisor with NULLIF to avoid divide-by-zero.

mistakes.m: Filtering grouped results with WHERE COUNT(*) > 1
mistakes.fix: Aggregates cannot go in WHERE (it runs before aggregation); filter groups with HAVING and rows with WHERE.

links.t: PostgreSQL: Window function calls (ROW_NUMBER, LAG, DENSE_RANK)

quiz.q: The standard pattern for 'latest row per user' (keeping all columns) is:
opts: SELECT DISTINCT user_id FROM events
ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY event_time DESC), then keep rn = 1
ORDER BY event_time DESC LIMIT 1
GROUP BY user_id with MAX(event_time) and nothing else
quiz.why: Partitioning per user and numbering by recency keeps exactly one full row per entity; a bare GROUP BY MAX(time) gives the latest time but not the other columns of that row without a self-join, LIMIT 1 returns one row for the whole table, and DISTINCT user_id drops every other column.
quiz.approach: PARTITION BY entity, ORDER BY recency DESC, filter the numbering = 1.

quiz.q: Why DENSE_RANK rather than ROW_NUMBER for 'second-highest salary'?
opts: It runs faster on large tables
ROW_NUMBER cannot be used inside a subquery
With ties, DENSE_RANK gives equal top salaries rank 1, so rank 2 is the true second distinct value
DENSE_RANK removes duplicate rows from the base table
quiz.why: If the top salary is tied, ROW_NUMBER labels them 1, 2, 3 and 'rank 2' returns a duplicate of the top; DENSE_RANK labels ties 1, 1, 2 so rank 2 is genuinely the second distinct value. It is not about speed, subquery rules, or de-duplicating the table.
quiz.approach: Use DENSE_RANK when you want the Nth distinct value in spite of ties.

quiz.q: Using LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month), the MoM % for the very first month is:
opts: Zero
NULL — there is no previous row to compare against
Equal to that month's revenue
A runtime error that aborts the query
quiz.why: LAG has no prior row at the start of the ordering, so it returns NULL; arithmetic involving NULL yields NULL rather than 0 or an error, which is why you guard the divisor with NULLIF and decide explicitly whether to COALESCE the edge.
quiz.approach: Expect NULL at series edges and guard the divisor (NULLIF) so the percentage stays clean.

lessons.title: Power BI, DAX Filter Context & the Dashboard Case Round
lessons.concept:
DAX lives or dies on context, and there are two kinds. Row context is a "current row" — present in calculated columns and inside iterators like SUMX. Filter context is the set of filters active when an expression is evaluated — coming from a visual's rows and columns, slicers, the filter pane, and CALCULATE. A measure has no row context of its own; it is evaluated purely in filter context. Interview line: "CALCULATE is the one function that modifies filter context, and when it runs inside a row context it performs context transition — turning the current row into an equivalent filter." The probe: "so why does referencing a measure inside SUMX still work?" — because a measure reference is implicitly wrapped in CALCULATE, which triggers context transition on the current iterated row. Gotcha / senior signal: saying "row context equals filter context" fails; naming context transition is the senior tell.

The classic DAX bug: Revenue = SUM(Qty) * SUM(Price) is wrong because it multiplies two grand totals. You need per-row arithmetic then a sum — SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[Price]). Related trap: totals in a matrix recompute at the total level, so a ratio measure (AOV, conversion %) shows the "sum of ratios does not equal ratio of sums" behaviour — the total row is the measure re-evaluated in the total's filter context, not a column sum. The probe: "why doesn't my percentage column total add up?" Gotcha / senior signal: use DIVIDE, not the / operator, so divide-by-zero (or divide-by-blank) returns BLANK safely instead of an error.

The dashboard case round: given "build a sales dashboard," the failing move is jumping to chart types. The senior sequence is (1) who is the user and what decision does this drive? (2) the 3-5 KPIs that answer it; (3) the grain and data model — a star schema with one date table; (4) layout — headline KPIs top-left, trend next, breakdowns and drill-through below, filters on the side; (5) definitions and data freshness. Interview line: "Before I pick visuals, who's using this and what will they do differently on Monday because of it?" The probe: "your KPI and finance's KPI disagree — why?" — differing definitions or filters, so you pin the metric once in the model. Gotcha / senior signal: 20 visuals with no hierarchy fails; a decision-first, few-KPI layout with agreed definitions impresses.
lessons.example: In a Swiggy-style case, "orders" can mean placed, confirmed, or delivered. A junior builds three charts; a senior first asks which one drives the ops decision, defines it once in the model, and lets every visual inherit that single definition.
lessons.code:
Total Revenue = SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[Price])   -- per-row, then sum

AOV = DIVIDE([Total Revenue], [Order Count])             -- safe divide -> BLANK on 0/blank

% of All Products =
DIVIDE(
    [Total Revenue],
    CALCULATE([Total Revenue], REMOVEFILTERS(Product))   -- denominator ignores Product filters
)
lessons.tryit: In Power BI Desktop, on any dataset, build two measures side by side in a matrix by category: a wrong one (SUM(Qty) * SUM(Price)) and the correct SUMX version — watch the totals diverge. Then write a five-line dashboard brief: user, decision, three KPIs, grain, and one risky definition to pin.

mistakes.m: Saying row context and filter context are the same thing
mistakes.fix: Row context is the current row (iterators, calculated columns); filter context is the active filters. CALCULATE bridges them via context transition.

mistakes.m: Computing revenue as SUM(Qty) * SUM(Price)
mistakes.fix: Use SUMX(Sales, Qty * Price) so the multiplication happens per row before summing, instead of multiplying two grand totals.

mistakes.m: Designing the dashboard by choosing charts first
mistakes.fix: Start with the user and the decision, define 3-5 KPIs and the grain, then lay out and pick visuals last.

links.t: Create measures in Power BI Desktop (Microsoft Learn)

quiz.q: In DAX, what does CALCULATE primarily do?
opts: Creates a new physical table in the model
Modifies the filter context in which its expression is evaluated
Sorts the visual it sits in
Triggers a scheduled data refresh
quiz.why: CALCULATE is the one function that changes filter context — adding, removing, or overriding filters — and when invoked inside a row context it performs context transition, turning the current row into a filter. It neither materialises tables, sorts visuals, nor refreshes data.
quiz.approach: Reach for CALCULATE whenever you need to add, remove, or override filters.

quiz.q: Why is Revenue = SUM(Sales[Qty]) * SUM(Sales[Price]) wrong for total revenue?
opts: SUM cannot be multiplied in DAX
It multiplies the two grand totals instead of summing Qty*Price per row; use SUMX(Sales, Qty*Price)
You must always wrap revenue in CALCULATE
Measures are not allowed to multiply columns
quiz.why: Revenue must be computed row by row (Qty times Price) and then summed; multiplying the two column totals inflates the figure, whereas SUMX iterates the table with a row context. SUM values can be multiplied and measures can multiply columns — the bug is the level at which the multiply happens.
quiz.approach: When a calc needs per-row arithmetic, iterate with an X-function (SUMX/AVERAGEX), never multiply aggregates.

quiz.q: Best first move when handed a dashboard case ('build a sales dashboard')?
opts: Pick chart types and a colour theme immediately
Ask who the user is and what decision it must drive, then define 3-5 KPIs and the grain
Load every available column so nothing is missed
Start with a pie chart covering everything
quiz.why: A dashboard is a decision tool; clarifying the user and the decision first determines the KPIs, grain and layout — visuals come last, not first. Choosing charts, dumping every column, or defaulting to a pie all skip the question the dashboard exists to answer.
quiz.approach: Decision and audience first, then KPIs and grain, then layout, then visuals.

lessons.title: Guesstimates, Product Sense, Smart Questions & Salary
lessons.concept:
Guesstimates test structure, not the number. Given "how many used cars are sold per month in India" or "daily cab rides in Bengaluru," the senior sequence is: clarify scope, choose top-down (population-based) or bottom-up (unit-based), state assumptions out loud, compute stepwise, sanity-check against something you already know, then name the assumption that most changes the answer. Interview line: "Let me build this top-down from population and state my assumptions as I go." The probe: "your number feels high — where's the error?" — so you pre-empt it with a sanity check. Gotcha / senior signal: blurting a single number with no structure or hiding assumptions fails; a clean, auditable estimate you can flex when a single input is challenged is the win.

Product sense goes beyond sizing: "how would you measure the success of feature X?" or "our DAU dropped 5%, what do you do?" Framework: pick one primary metric, add two guardrail metrics (do not win engagement by hurting retention or revenue), segment to localise a drop (new vs returning, platform, city, release date), and separate correlation from an A/B-tested cause. Interview line: "I'd pick one primary metric and two guardrails, then segment the drop before blaming the feature." The probe: "how would you know the feature caused it?" — a controlled A/B test with a pre-set significance threshold. Gotcha / senior signal: proposing ten metrics with no primary fails; one primary plus guardrails is the senior tell.

Your questions to the interviewer are themselves an answer. Strong ones: "what decision has the team recently changed because of data?", "how is success in this role measured at six months?", "what's the data stack and how mature is self-serve?" — save leave policy for HR. Then salary, in Indian CTC reality: know fixed vs variable vs ESOP, do not volunteer a low current CTC as the recruiter's anchor, quote a researched market range, and negotiate total comp (a joining bonus can offset a notice-period buyout or lapsed ESOPs). Interview line to the recruiter: "I'm targeting X-Y fixed based on market and my scope; happy to discuss the full structure." The probe: "what's your current CTC?" — deflect to expectations and market. Gotcha / senior signal: accepting on the call or anchoring on your old salary fails; getting the offer in writing and negotiating total comp impresses.
lessons.example: Bengaluru cab rides, top-down: roughly 13 million people, say ~40% are working-age app users (about 5.2M), ~10% take a cab on a given day (about 520k riders), ~1.4 rides each — roughly 0.7 million rides/day. Then sanity-check whether that is plausible for one metro, and note that the daily ride-rate assumption drives the answer most, so you flag it as the number to pressure-test.
lessons.code:
GUESSTIMATE:
  1 Clarify scope (geo, time window, definition)
  2 Pick approach: top-down (population) or bottom-up (units)
  3 State assumptions explicitly (write them down)
  4 Compute step by step
  5 Sanity-check vs a known anchor
  6 Name the assumption that moves the answer most

PRODUCT METRIC:
  Primary   -> the one number success is measured by
  Guardrails-> retention, revenue, latency (don't break these)
  Segment   -> new/returning, platform, city, release date
  Cause     -> A/B test w/ control + significance, not correlation
lessons.tryit: Estimate monthly used-car sales in India two ways — top-down (population and car-ownership) and bottom-up (dealers times cars per dealer) — and reconcile the gap. Separately, write three interview-closing questions and one scripted line to deflect the "current CTC?" question.

mistakes.m: Answering a guesstimate with a single number and no structure
mistakes.fix: State your approach, voice each assumption, compute stepwise, then sanity-check — the structure is what's scored, not the number.

mistakes.m: Listing ten success metrics with no primary
mistakes.fix: Name one primary metric plus two guardrails, then segment before attributing cause.

mistakes.m: Volunteering your current CTC as the anchor
mistakes.fix: Quote a researched market range, negotiate total comp, and decide only once the offer is in writing.

quiz.q: The cleanest way to open a guesstimate ('used cars sold per month in India')?
opts: State a single number confidently and move on
Say it can't be done without exact data
Lay out a structure (top-down or bottom-up), state assumptions, compute, then sanity-check
Ask to look it up online
quiz.why: Guesstimates score your structure and explicit assumptions, not the final number; a stated, auditable method you can flex under challenge is the win. A bare number, refusing, or reaching for a lookup all skip the reasoning the question is testing.
quiz.approach: Clarify, pick top-down or bottom-up, voice each assumption, compute, sanity-check, name the biggest driver.

quiz.q: A PM asks you to explain a 5% DAU drop. The senior approach is:
opts: Immediately blame the latest feature release
Add ten new metrics to the dashboard
Pick one primary metric and guardrails, segment the drop (new/returning, platform, release date), then A/B test for cause
Wait for the number to recover on its own
quiz.why: Localising the drop by segment and separating correlation from an A/B-tested cause beats guessing; one primary metric plus guardrails keeps the analysis honest. Blaming the release, drowning in metrics, or waiting all avoid the actual diagnosis.
quiz.approach: One primary metric, two guardrails, segment first, then prove cause with a controlled test.

quiz.q: In an Indian salary negotiation, a common anchoring mistake is:
opts: Asking for the offer in writing before deciding
Volunteering a low current CTC that becomes the recruiter's anchor for your new offer
Researching market ranges beforehand
Negotiating on total compensation, not just base
quiz.why: Recruiters routinely peg the new offer to your last-drawn CTC, so leading with a low number caps your upside; anchor on a researched market range and total comp instead. Asking for a written offer, researching ranges, and negotiating total comp are all good practice, not mistakes.
quiz.approach: Deflect the current-CTC question, anchor to market, negotiate total comp, and decide only on a written offer.
